item.title: Best Prime Day deals on power banks and chargers
item.itunes:title: Best Prime Day deals on power banks and chargers
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Quick Links

  • Will there be a second Prime Day in 2023?
  • Prime Big Deal Days vs. Prime Day: What’s the difference?
  • Do you need to be a Prime member to save on Prime Big Deal Days?

When it comes to major sales events, Amazon pretty much has the market cornered. Its Prime Day event has traditionally only occurred once per year, but due to the popularity Amazon has been ramping things up to bring us more great deals throughout the year. Last year's Prime Early Access Sale was the first time that Amazon hosted two events in one year, and this year we're expecting another Prime Big Deal Days event in the first half of October. This will be the third time that Amazon has hosted a sales event in October, getting many people set up early for the holiday season.

A popular category that I always keep an eye on during Amazon's Prime sales events are chargers and power banks. These accessories seem to be a shoo-in for deep sales every year, and that should hold true again for Amazon's Prime Big Deal Days event that's expected to run in the first half of October.

I can never seem to have enough power banks around the house for when I'm traveling, cycling, camping, hiking, or visiting a coffee shop to work on an article, and I don't think I've ever paid full price. Good power banks and chargers don't usually don't come cheap, but Amazon's Prime sales events are generally a perfect time to find new low prices on the best portable chargers and power banks out there right now.

Will there be a second Prime Day in 2023?

Amazon is expected to host another huge sales event in the first half of October. The Amazon Prime Big Deal Days still doesn't have concrete dates, but we know it's coming soon.

It's only the second time that Amazon has hosted a second Prime sales event — with the first being last year's Prime Early Access Sale — and it's the third time that Amazon has scheduled a sales event in October. This should give shoppers more than a month's head start on Black Friday and Cyber Monday holiday shopping in November, in which Amazon also plays a huge role.

Prime Big Deal Days vs. Prime Day: What’s the difference?

Aside from the naming change-up, Amazon's Prime Big Deal Days is expected in October instead of earlier in the year when the Prime Day event is usually held.

We're expecting to see many great deals that will slide us right into Black Friday and Cyber Monday holiday shopping. If you hate waiting until November to cash in on deals, The Prime Big Deal Days event should be a great alternative. It's expected that pretty much every shopping category will have great deals, just like during the standard Prime Day.

Do you need to be a Prime member to save on Prime Big Deal Days?

Amazon's Prime Big Deal Days is primarily reserved for Prime members. This shouldn't come as a surprise, and Prime members will be able to jump in and start saving immediately. You will likely come across some deals on Amazon that don't require a Prime membership, but the best deals will be gated.

If you'd rather not sign up for a Prime membership in order to take advantage of the savings, most other major retailers are expected to host competing sales events to draw a bit of traffic away from Amazon.

Amazon Prime

Looking to save during Amazon's Prime Big Deal Days event? A Prime subscription can go a long way past just free shipping and big savings, with everything from music, movies, games, and storage at your fingertips.

Sign up for Amazon Prime

FAQ

Q: What charging capacity do you need?

Most power banks that you come across will have a capacity rated in milliampere hours (mAh). A 10,000mAh capacity is very common, and it will be enough to charge many phones two times. The charging capacity also generally reflects the size of the power bank itself. You'll be able to carry anything less than 20,000mAh comfortably, but beyond that, things can start to get bulky. On that note, if you're buying a charger for a laptop, you'll want to start at 20,000mAh and go up from there. Just be sure you don't go beyond about 26,500mAh, which comes out close to the maximum 100Wh capacity allowed on commercial flights.

Q: Do you need fast charging?

Most modern phones come with some sort of fast charging capabilities, and you should always try to find a power bank or charger that can accommodate. For example, iPhones support Power Delivery at about 20W, while some Android phones go with proprietary tech that pushes the speed beyond 20W. Check what standard your device uses and find a charger that can keep up. You'll be much happier in the long run even if you spend a few more dollars.

Q: What power output should I look out for?

A lot of modern laptops require somewhere between 45W and 65W of charging power, but that number can creep up if you're dealing with a system that includes a discrete GPU. Whether you're buying a power bank or charger for a phone or a laptop, you should always try to match or exceed the ability of the charger that came with your device. Buying a 45W charger for a laptop that requires 60W to run won't work very well. If you're buying a replacement or secondary wall charger, Gallium Nitride (GaN) technology is a bonus. The chargers are generally more compact and more efficient, but they do usually cost more.

Q: Do I need specific ports?

All power banks and chargers come with some sort of ports to connect to your devices, and you want to be sure that you're getting the right ones for your products. I have some power banks that require dongles, and I'd be lying if I said I hadn't misplaced them when needed most. The most common ports are USB-A and USB-C, which cover most modern tech devices. For maximum convenience, grab a power bank that can charge the same way it delivers power to reduce the amount of cables you carry around. And if you're shopping for a new wall charger, something without fixed cables is always a better buy. You can replace the cable easily if it receives damage

item.title: Best motherboards for gaming in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best motherboards for gaming in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The motherboard is an important part of any PC, especially a system geared towards gaming. There are various factors to consider such as the socket, chipset, storage interfaces, headers/ports, and RAM support. The more money spent on the motherboard, the better the feature set and overclocking capabilities. The processor, be it AMD or Intel, is then able to interface with the motherboard and play all your favorite PC games. Here are our recommendations for the best gaming motherboard.

  • Source: ASUS
    Asus ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming
    Best AMD gaming motherboard
    $480 at Amazon
  • Source: Gigabyte
    Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master
    Best Intel gaming motherboard
    $460 at Amazon
  • Source: Asus
    Asus TUF Gaming B650-Plus WiFi
    Best budget AMD gaming motherboard
    $220 at Amazon
  • Source: ASRock
    ASRock B660M Pro RS
    Best budget Intel gaming motherboard
    $115 at Amazon
  • Source: ASUS
    ASUS ROG Strix X670E-I Gaming WIFI
    Best compact AMD gaming motherboard
    $450 at Amazon
  • Source: ASRock
    ASRock Z790M-ITX WiFi
    Best compact Intel gaming motherboard
    $222 at Amazon

Our top gaming motherboard picks for 2023

Source: ASUS
Asus ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming
Best AMD gaming motherboard

This is the gaming motherboard to buy for AMD CPUs

The Asus ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming is a high-end Asus motherboard that supports PCIe 5.0 graphics, three PCIe 5.0 SSDs, and high-end Ryzen 7000 CPUs thanks to its large 18+2 stage VRM.

Memory Type
DDR5-6400+
Form Factor
ATX
CPU Support
AMD Ryzen 7000+
Chipset
X670
Socket
AM5
Power Phases
18+2
PCI Slots
2x PCIe 5.0 x16, 1x PCie 4.0 x16
Storage
4x SATA, 2x PCIe 5.0 M.2, 2x PCIe 4.0 M.2
Audio
Realtek ALC4080
Networking
Intel 2.5Gb, 2x2 Wi-Fi 6E (802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax)
Internal I/O
1x USB-C 3.2 Gen 2x2, 1x USB-A 3.2 Gen 1, 3x USB-A 2.0, 1x Thunderbolt header
Rear I/O
1x USB-C 3.2 Gen 2x2 port, 10x USB-A 3.2 Gen 2 ports, 2x USB-C 3.2 Gen 2 ports
Pros
  • Solid connectivity
  • Excellent overall performance
Cons
  • Pricey
$480 at Amazon $480 at Newegg $480 at B&H

This motherboard from ASUS does it all for gaming PC builds. The ASUS ROG Strix X670-E Gaming is quite the board, supporting AMD Ryzen 7000 processors and DDR5 RAM. Then there's PCIe 5.0 support for expansion and storage, so you can connect the fastest SSDs and most powerful graphics cards. To make all the magic happen with an AMD Ryzen 9 7950X, ASUS implemented its tried and tested 18+2 stage power design for exceptional reliability, even when the system is under intense loads. You can install up to 128GB of DDR5-6400 RAM. There are two x16 slots that are capable of running up to PCIe 5.0 and both are armored for even the heaviest of graphics cards.

M.2 storage is a mix of PCIe 5.0 and PCIe 4.0 and there are four slots in total, allowing you to completely switch to rapid NVMe SSDs for not only the OS but all your favorite PC games. And because we're talking gamer-centric components, ASUS made sure this Republic of Gamers product looks the part with a sleek dark design and some RGB lighting. Some notable features for system builders include the fan and water cooling headers and an LED code display in the top right-hand corner. There are also more ports on this motherboard than you'll know what to do with, including Wi-Fi 6E and 2.5G Ethernet.

Source: Gigabyte
Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master
Best Intel gaming motherboard

This is a beastly gaming motherboard for Intel systems

$460 $490 Save $30

There's very little to dislike about the Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master aside from the price. It's expensive, but you get a lot of features, including solid power delivery, excellent BIOS, and plenty of connections for all your components.

Our top pick for the best gaming Intel motherboard is the mighty Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master. It strikes a solid balance between price, features, and performance, just like our top AMD board recommendation. Rocking an LGA 1700 socket, this motherboard is capable of handling 12th and 13th Gen processors, including the incredible Intel Core i9-13900K. Like other Intel and AMD consumer-grade motherboards you're looking at four DIMM slots (maximum capacity of 128GB) with support for up to DDR5-8000. While Intel does support DDR4 with its 12th and 13th Gen processors, you'll need to pick another board if you'd like to continue using an existing kit.

One x16 PCIe 5.0 slot is available, which is all you'll need for a graphics card, but Gigabyte made sure that one of five available M.2 slots shared the same generation. The remaining four PCIe slots for M.2 SSDs are PCIe 4.0, which can still support NVMe storage with ridiculous speeds. Just like other LGA 1700 boards with the Z790 chipset, you'll get all the usual connectors and headers to connect all your external peripherals including fans, pumps, RGB lights, peripherals, and more.

The ASRock B660M Pro RS is a considerably more affordable motherboard than our top Intel pick and it shows in the specification list, as well as the overall aesthetic. This chipset launched alongside the 12th Gen Intel processors and would work well with either one of those or the newer 13th Gen successors. Compared to our top Z790 Intel motherboard recommendation, you're losing out on PCIe 5.0 support altogether with the two x16 slots supporting PCIe 4.0 only. There's only support for DDR4 RAM here with speeds up to 4800MT/s. That shouldn't be an issue for gaming, but it's worth bearing in mind if you want to build a new PC with the very latest hardware.

Even the two M.2 storage slots are only PCIe 4.0 and PCIe 3.0. Again, this is only really going to play a factor when you comfortably reach the transfer speed limit of PCIe 4.0, which won't likely happen too frequently (if at all) when gaming. The power delivery on this board isn't going to be great for overclocking a power-hungry CPU, but you'll be absolutely fine when keeping expectations in check. It's a little understated in terms of looks, but ASRock does offer a few ways to hook up your favorite LED strips and other lighting accessories for easier management and synchronizing of effects.

Should you have a compact gaming PC or a case that's a little on the small side, a Mini-ITX motherboard could be the only option. For AMD builds, we'd recommend the ASUS ROG Strix X670E-I Gaming WIFI. It's a premium motherboard with some impressive features for its form factor. Despite its size, ASUS managed to include a chunky M.2 thermal guard to help with SSD performance, as well as a multi-layered heatsink design. The 10+2 phase design for power delivery is actually better than many budget ATX motherboards and shows just how capable this tiny board is. Fully supporting AMD Ryzen 7000 series processors, you could get away with installing an AMD Ryzen 9 7950X without much issue, so long as you keep everything in the BIOS relating to CPU overclocking in a stock configuration.

Because we're going to be playing PC games, it's important we have a full-size PCI slot and the ASUS ROG Strix X670E-I Gaming does not disappoint. Only two DIMM slots are available, however reducing the maximum supported capacity to 64GB. This motherboard also supports Wi-Fi 6, making it a future-proof option for those not wanting to deal with Ethernet all the time, but a 2.5Gb connection is available if cables are preferred. As for the ports, the ASUS ROG Strix X670E-I Gaming WIFI offers an HDMI port, two USB-C 4.0 ports, five USB-A 3.2 Gen 2 ports, three USB-A 2.0 ports, the aforementioned Ethernet, and audio jacks. Additionally, there are plenty of I/O connectors on the board for fans, extra USBs, an AIO pump, and more.

Our compact motherboard recommendation for Intel processors would be the ASRock Z790M-ITX WiFi. It's a Mini-ITX motherboard and is considerably smaller than a full-size ATX board, allowing you to install it inside even the more compact PC chassis. It's small, but a mighty foundation for 12th and 13th Gen Intel processors. ASRock reinforced the PCIe 5.0 x16 slot for GPUs, which should help avoid sag and damage to larger cards. The primary M.2 slot is shielded by a heatsink that also doubles up as a channel for heat to dissipate from the M.2 SSD. A second M.2 slot is at hand for completely converting to speedier NVMe storage.

For power delivery, ASRock makes full use of a 7+1+1 phase design. It's nowhere near the best on the market, but it's more than powerful enough for even an Intel Core i9-13900K. You may find the two DIMM slots a little limiting by only supporting up to 64GB of DDR5-7000 but even that capacity should be enough for heavier games. Wi-Fi 6 joins 2.5Gb networking and for USB ports, ASRock provides a single USB-C 3.2 Gen2x2 port, four USB-A 3.2 Gen 2 ports, and a single USB-A 2.0 port.

What is the best gaming motherboard?

Gaming can prove to be an intensive task for the processor, graphics card, as well as other components. You need to choose wisely since you'll need to ensure you can run your GPU at maximum speed, as well as your storage and system memory for the best in-game results. But really, so long as you're matching components for compatibility, you'll be good to go. We'd recommend the ASUS ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming for AMD processors and Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master for Intel chips.

We've compiled a list of more affordable and compact motherboards too, just in case you want to save a little or are tight on internal PC case space. The most important factors to consider are the socket type, chipset, memory support, and connectivity. These are what will affect your PC build the most with socket type and chipset largely determining the processors you should install and use. Our round-up of the best motherboards will prove to be a solid foundation for an excellent gaming PC.

Source: ASUS

There are various factors to consider such as the socket, chipset, storage interfaces, headers/ports, and RAM support. The more money spent on the motherboard, the better the feature set and overclocking capabilities. The processor, be it AMD or Intel, is then able to interface with the motherboard and play all your favorite PC games. Here are our recommendations for the best gaming motherboard.

This motherboard from ASUS does it all for gaming PC builds. The ASUS ROG Strix X670-E Gaming is quite the board, supporting AMD Ryzen 7000 processors and DDR5 RAM. Then there's PCIe 5.0 support for expansion and storage, so you can connect the fastest SSDs and most powerful graphics cards. To make all the magic happen with an AMD Ryzen 9 7950X, ASUS implemented its tried and tested 18+2 stage power design for exceptional reliability, even when the system is under intense loads. You can install up to 128GB of DDR5-6400 RAM. There are two x16 slots that are capable of running up to PCIe 5.0 and both are armored for even the heaviest of graphics cards.

M.2 storage is a mix of PCIe 5.0 and PCIe 4.0 and there are four slots in total, allowing you to completely switch to rapid NVMe SSDs for not only the OS but all your favorite PC games. And because we're talking gamer-centric components, ASUS made sure this Republic of Gamers product looks the part with a sleek dark design and some RGB lighting. Some notable features for system builders include the fan and water cooling headers and an LED code display in the top right-hand corner. There are also more ports on this motherboard than you'll know what to do with, including Wi-Fi 6E and 2.5G Ethernet.

You need to choose wisely since you'll need to ensure you can run your GPU at maximum speed, as well as your storage and system memory for the best in-game results. But really, so long as you're matching components for compatibility, you'll be good to go. We'd recommend the ASUS ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming for AMD processors and Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master for Intel chips.

We've compiled a list of more affordable and compact motherboards too, just in case you want to save a little or are tight on internal PC case space. The most important factors to consider are the socket type, chipset, memory support, and connectivity. These are what will affect your PC build the most with socket type and chipset largely determining the processors you should install and use. Our round-up of the best motherboards will prove to be a solid foundation for an excellent gaming PC.

Source: ASUS
Asus ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming
Best AMD gaming motherboard

The Asus ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming is a high-end Asus motherboard that supports PCIe 5.0 graphics, three PCIe 5.0 SSDs, and high-end Ryzen 7000 CPUs thanks to its large 18+2 stage VRM.

$480 at Amazon $480 at Newegg $480 at B&H
Source: Gigabyte
Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master
Best Intel gaming motherboard
$460 $490 Save $30

There's very little to dislike about the Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master aside from the price. It's expensive, but you get a lot of features, including solid power delivery, excellent BIOS, and plenty of connections for all your components.

$460 at Amazon $490 at Newegg
item.content:encoded:
The motherboard is an important part of any PC, especially a system geared towards gaming. There are various factors to consider such as the socket, chipset, storage interfaces, headers/ports, and RAM support. The more money spent on the motherboard, the better the feature set and overclocking capabilities. The processor, be it AMD or Intel, is then able to interface with the motherboard and play all your favorite PC games. Here are our recommendations for the best gaming motherboard.

  • Source: ASUS
    Asus ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming
    Best AMD gaming motherboard
    $480 at Amazon
  • Source: Gigabyte
    Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master
    Best Intel gaming motherboard
    $460 at Amazon
  • Source: Asus
    Asus TUF Gaming B650-Plus WiFi
    Best budget AMD gaming motherboard
    $220 at Amazon
  • Source: ASRock
    ASRock B660M Pro RS
    Best budget Intel gaming motherboard
    $115 at Amazon
  • Source: ASUS
    ASUS ROG Strix X670E-I Gaming WIFI
    Best compact AMD gaming motherboard
    $450 at Amazon
  • Source: ASRock
    ASRock Z790M-ITX WiFi
    Best compact Intel gaming motherboard
    $222 at Amazon

Our top gaming motherboard picks for 2023

Source: ASUS
Asus ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming
Best AMD gaming motherboard

This is the gaming motherboard to buy for AMD CPUs

The Asus ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming is a high-end Asus motherboard that supports PCIe 5.0 graphics, three PCIe 5.0 SSDs, and high-end Ryzen 7000 CPUs thanks to its large 18+2 stage VRM.

Memory Type

1x USB-C 3.2 Gen 2x2 port, 10x USB-A 3.2 Gen 2 ports, 2x USB-C 3.2 Gen 2 ports
Pros
  • Solid connectivity
  • Excellent overall performance
Cons
  • Pricey
$480 at Amazon $480 at Newegg $480 at B&H

This motherboard from ASUS does it all for gaming PC builds. The ASUS ROG Strix X670-E Gaming is quite the board, supporting AMD Ryzen 7000 processors and DDR5 RAM. Then there's PCIe 5.0 support for expansion and storage, so you can connect the fastest SSDs and most powerful graphics cards. To make all the magic happen with an AMD Ryzen 9 7950X, ASUS implemented its tried and tested 18+2 stage power design for exceptional reliability, even when the system is under intense loads. You can install up to 128GB of DDR5-6400 RAM. There are two x16 slots that are capable of running up to PCIe 5.0 and both are armored for even the heaviest of graphics cards.

M.2 storage is a mix of PCIe 5.0 and PCIe 4.0 and there are four slots in total, allowing you to completely switch to rapid NVMe SSDs for not only the OS but all your favorite PC games. And because we're talking gamer-centric components, ASUS made sure this Republic of Gamers product looks the part with a sleek dark design and some RGB lighting. Some notable features for system builders include the fan and water cooling headers and an LED code display in the top right-hand corner. There are also more ports on this motherboard than you'll know what to do with, including Wi-Fi 6E and 2.5G Ethernet.

Source: Gigabyte
Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master
Best Intel gaming motherboard

This is a beastly gaming motherboard for Intel systems

$460 $490 Save $30

There's very little to dislike about the Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master aside from the price. It's expensive, but you get a lot of features, including solid power delivery, excellent BIOS, and plenty of connections for all your components.

Brand
Gigabyte
Memory Slots
4

Rear I/O
1x USB-C 3.2 Gen 1, 2x USB-C 3.2 Gen 2x2, 7x USB-A 3.2 Gen 2, 4x USB-A 3.2 Gen 1, 1x DisplayPort
Pros
  • Great overclocking support
  • Excellent performance
Cons
  • Pricey
$460 at Amazon $490 at Newegg

Our top pick for the best gaming Intel motherboard is the mighty Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master. It strikes a solid balance between price, features, and performance, just like our top AMD board recommendation. Rocking an LGA 1700 socket, this motherboard is capable of handling 12th and 13th Gen processors, including the incredible Intel Core i9-13900K. Like other Intel and AMD consumer-grade motherboards you're looking at four DIMM slots (maximum capacity of 128GB) with support for up to DDR5-8000. While Intel does support DDR4 with its 12th and 13th Gen processors, you'll need to pick another board if you'd like to continue using an existing kit.

One x16 PCIe 5.0 slot is available, which is all you'll need for a graphics card, but Gigabyte made sure that one of five available M.2 slots shared the same generation. The remaining four PCIe slots for M.2 SSDs are PCIe 4.0, which can still support NVMe storage with ridiculous speeds. Just like other LGA 1700 boards with the Z790 chipset, you'll get all the usual connectors and headers to connect all your external peripherals including fans, pumps, RGB lights, peripherals, and more.

Source: Asus
Asus TUF Gaming B650-Plus WiFi
Best budget AMD gaming motherboard

Save a little on this very capable AMD platform

The Asus TUF Gaming B650-Plus WiFi is a motherboard with plenty going for it. It supports DDR5 RAM, PCIe 5.0 SSDs, and manages to cut a few coins by restricting GPU connectivity to just PCIe 4.0.

Pros
  • Good connectivity
  • Good value
Cons
  • Limited overclocking support
$220 at Amazon $220 at Newegg

You don't need much for a gaming PC build, which is what makes the ASUS TUF Gaming B650-PLUS worth considering for AMD Ryzen 7000 processors. It has an AM5 socket and may be more affordable compared to the X690 chipset, but you will be sacrificing a few PCIe lanes and connectivity options. This will have zero impact on gaming performance. For instance, ASUS only included two PCIe 4.0 x16 slots with no option for PCIe 5.0. There is a single PCIe 5.0 M.2 slot for NVMe SSDs, however. This is joined by a further two PCIe 4.0 M.2 slots and a few SATA III ports.

While this motherboard does cut down on a few features you'll likely not require, the flip side is ASUS allows the installation of up to 128GB of DDR5-6400 RAM. Other handy features of the ASUS TUF Gaming B650-PLUS motherboard include Wi-Fi 6 and 2.5Gb networking for super fast and reliable connections. Should you wish to save a little on space inside the PC chassis, the ASUS TUF Gaming B650M-PLUS is a smaller micro-ATX motherboard that costs a little less and drops a few features.

Source: ASRock
ASRock B660M Pro RS
Best budget Intel gaming motherboard

A great motherboard at a reasonable price

$115 $150 Save $35

The ASRock B660M Pro RS is a solid B660-based motherboard for budget-centric Alder Lake builds. it supports DDR4 memory modules and comes with all the essential features to power Alder Lake chips.

Memory Slots
4

DDR4-5333

Pros
  • Affordable
  • Good connectivity
Cons
  • Limited overclocking support
  • DDR4 RAM support
$115 at Amazon $120 at Newegg

The ASRock B660M Pro RS is a considerably more affordable motherboard than our top Intel pick and it shows in the specification list, as well as the overall aesthetic. This chipset launched alongside the 12th Gen Intel processors and would work well with either one of those or the newer 13th Gen successors. Compared to our top Z790 Intel motherboard recommendation, you're losing out on PCIe 5.0 support altogether with the two x16 slots supporting PCIe 4.0 only. There's only support for DDR4 RAM here with speeds up to 4800MT/s. That shouldn't be an issue for gaming, but it's worth bearing in mind if you want to build a new PC with the very latest hardware.

Even the two M.2 storage slots are only PCIe 4.0 and PCIe 3.0. Again, this is only really going to play a factor when you comfortably reach the transfer speed limit of PCIe 4.0, which won't likely happen too frequently (if at all) when gaming. The power delivery on this board isn't going to be great for overclocking a power-hungry CPU, but you'll be absolutely fine when keeping expectations in check. It's a little understated in terms of looks, but ASRock does offer a few ways to hook up your favorite LED strips and other lighting accessories for easier management and synchronizing of effects.

Source: ASUS
ASUS ROG Strix X670E-I Gaming WIFI
Best compact AMD gaming motherboard

For those wanting to build a powerful compact AMD PC

$450 $470 Save $20

This compact ITX ASUS ROG Strix X670E motherboard has everything you'll need for a powerful PC that can fit inside a smaller form factor chassis.

$450 at Amazon $470 at Newegg

Should you have a compact gaming PC or a case that's a little on the small side, a Mini-ITX motherboard could be the only option. For AMD builds, we'd recommend the ASUS ROG Strix X670E-I Gaming WIFI. It's a premium motherboard with some impressive features for its form factor. Despite its size, ASUS managed to include a chunky M.2 thermal guard to help with SSD performance, as well as a multi-layered heatsink design. The 10+2 phase design for power delivery is actually better than many budget ATX motherboards and shows just how capable this tiny board is. Fully supporting AMD Ryzen 7000 series processors, you could get away with installing an AMD Ryzen 9 7950X without much issue, so long as you keep everything in the BIOS relating to CPU overclocking in a stock configuration.

Because we're going to be playing PC games, it's important we have a full-size PCI slot and the ASUS ROG Strix X670E-I Gaming does not disappoint. Only two DIMM slots are available, however reducing the maximum supported capacity to 64GB. This motherboard also supports Wi-Fi 6, making it a future-proof option for those not wanting to deal with Ethernet all the time, but a 2.5Gb connection is available if cables are preferred. As for the ports, the ASUS ROG Strix X670E-I Gaming WIFI offers an HDMI port, two USB-C 4.0 ports, five USB-A 3.2 Gen 2 ports, three USB-A 2.0 ports, the aforementioned Ethernet, and audio jacks. Additionally, there are plenty of I/O connectors on the board for fans, extra USBs, an AIO pump, and more.

Source: ASRock
ASRock Z790M-ITX WiFi
Best compact Intel gaming motherboard

Build an Xbox-inspired Intel gaming PC with this motherboard

This compact ASRock motherboard is brilliant for a small gaming PC. It has the flagship Intel Z790 chipset and the LGA 1700 socket for 12th and 13th Gen processors.

$222 at Amazon $222 at Newegg

Our compact motherboard recommendation for Intel processors would be the ASRock Z790M-ITX WiFi. It's a Mini-ITX motherboard and is considerably smaller than a full-size ATX board, allowing you to install it inside even the more compact PC chassis. It's small, but a mighty foundation for 12th and 13th Gen Intel processors. ASRock reinforced the PCIe 5.0 x16 slot for GPUs, which should help avoid sag and damage to larger cards. The primary M.2 slot is shielded by a heatsink that also doubles up as a channel for heat to dissipate from the M.2 SSD. A second M.2 slot is at hand for completely converting to speedier NVMe storage.

For power delivery, ASRock makes full use of a 7+1+1 phase design. It's nowhere near the best on the market, but it's more than powerful enough for even an Intel Core i9-13900K. You may find the two DIMM slots a little limiting by only supporting up to 64GB of DDR5-7000 but even that capacity should be enough for heavier games. Wi-Fi 6 joins 2.5Gb networking and for USB ports, ASRock provides a single USB-C 3.2 Gen2x2 port, four USB-A 3.2 Gen 2 ports, and a single USB-A 2.0 port.

What is the best gaming motherboard?

Gaming can prove to be an intensive task for the processor, graphics card, as well as other components. You need to choose wisely since you'll need to ensure you can run your GPU at maximum speed, as well as your storage and system memory for the best in-game results. But really, so long as you're matching components for compatibility, you'll be good to go. We'd recommend the ASUS ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming for AMD processors and Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master for Intel chips.

We've compiled a list of more affordable and compact motherboards too, just in case you want to save a little or are tight on internal PC case space. The most important factors to consider are the socket type, chipset, memory support, and connectivity. These are what will affect your PC build the most with socket type and chipset largely determining the processors you should install and use. Our round-up of the best motherboards will prove to be a solid foundation for an excellent gaming PC.

Source: ASUS
Asus ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming
Best AMD gaming motherboard

The Asus ROG Strix X670E-E Gaming is a high-end Asus motherboard that supports PCIe 5.0 graphics, three PCIe 5.0 SSDs, and high-end Ryzen 7000 CPUs thanks to its large 18+2 stage VRM.

$480 at Amazon $480 at Newegg $480 at B&H
Source: Gigabyte
Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master
Best Intel gaming motherboard
$460 $490 Save $30

There's very little to dislike about the Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Master aside from the price. It's expensive, but you get a lot of features, including solid power delivery, excellent BIOS, and plenty of connections for all your components.

$460 at Amazon $490 at Newegg

item.title: Best PCIe 5.0 SSDs for 2023
item.itunes:title: Best PCIe 5.0 SSDs for 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The newest, fastest standard for M.2 SSDs, PCIe 5.0, has been around for a couple of years, but only this year brought a consumer release for the fastest M.2 SSDs. The first crop of M.2 NVMe SSDs using this standard promise speeds of 10,000MB/s or higher. When the second wave comes, expect speeds of 16,000MB/s, close to the theoretical limits of the PCIe 5.0 specification.

The only PCIe 5.0 SSDs available right now are from the first wave, and we’ve collected them all in one place to help you make your choice. Just keep in mind that you need a motherboard that supports PCIe 5.0, which is available on AMD’s AM5 platform, or some Intel motherboards that support 12-gen Intel CPUs and beyond.

  • Source: Crucial
    Crucial T700 NVMe SSD
    Editor's choice
    $160 at Amazon
  • Source: MSI
    MSI Spatium M570 PCIe 5.0 NVMe
    Premium pick
    $270 at Amazon
  • Source: Gigabyte
    Gigabyte Aorus Gen5 10000
    Best value
    $290 at Newegg
  • Source: Inland
    Inland TD510 PCIe 5.0 NVMe
    Best active cooling
    $154 at Amazon
  • Source: Nextorage Japan
    Nextorage Japan NVMe M.2 PCIe Gen5
    Best heatsink
    $170 at Newegg
  • Source: Seagate
    Seagate FireCuda 540 PCIe Gen5
    Best for longevity
    $180 at B&H
  • Source: Corsair
    Corsair MP700 PCIe Gen5
    Best customer support
    $150 at Newegg
  • Source: fanxiang
    Fanxiang S900 PCIe Gen5
    Best no-frills
    $190 at Amazon

These are the best PCIe 5.0 SSDs to buy in 2023

Source: Crucial
Crucial T700 NVMe SSD
Editor's choice

The fastest consumer SSD (for now)

$160 $180 Save $20

The Crucial T700 PCIe Gen5 NVMe SSD is one of the fastest solid-state drives on the planet, with up to 11,700MB/s reads and 9,500MB/s writes on the 1TB capacity, increasing to 12,400MB/s reads and 11,800MB/s writes on the larger capacities.

Pros
  • Blisteringly fast transfer speeds
  • Supports Microsoft DirectStorage
  • Optional heatsink
Cons
  • Not many devices support PCIe Gen 5 yet
  • Expensive compared to PCIe Gen 4 drives
$160 at Amazon $160 at Best Buy $160 at Newegg

The Crucial T700 is the best PCIe 5.0 SSD you can buy right now. That’s partly because it’s the fastest drive on the market, with 11,700MB/s max read speeds and 9,500MB/s write speeds in sequential transfers. It’s also partly because it’s one of the cheaper drives on the market, only a few dollars more per drive than the most affordable PCIe NVMe SSDs. Our testing showed max speeds of 12,398 MB/s reads and 11,814 MB/s writes, higher than Crucial's own seemingly conservative marketing numbers.

The drive comes in an M.2 2280 single-side form factor with an NVMe controller, a DRAM chip, and two flash packages. Those flash packages use Micron’s 232-layer TLC NAND3, which has a maximum speed of 2.4GB/s. Crucial has the edge as Micron is its parent company and gets access to the newer technology first, so it can optimize its SSDs accordingly.

The T700 drive is available in capacities of 1TB, 2TB, and 4TB, with or without heatsinks. We should mention that PCIe 5.0 SSDs run hot, and you will want a heatsink for them to work properly. The options with already-attached heatsinks are for motherboards that don’t already have onboard metal heatsinks to keep things cool. We noticed in our testing that the drive starts to thermal throttle under peak loads, with a temperature of 81 degrees Celsius. You'll want to keep this spicy drive well-cooled.

The drive also supports Microsoft’s DirectStorage, which enables your graphics card and SSD to transfer data to each other without it first going through the CPU. This reduces loading times for textures and other assets while lowering CPU utilization. Only one current game supports this, Forsaken, but others are coming soon. It's backed by a five-year warranty, which seems to be the standard for this gen.

Source: MSI
MSI Spatium M570 PCIe 5.0 NVMe
Premium pick

Eye-catching design

$270 $350 Save $80

The MSI Spatium M570 PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD is a premium M.2 SSD with blisteringly fast 10,000MB/s read and write speeds using the PCIe 5.0 standard. It comes with a chunky aluminum heatsink to keep things cool, though you pay a premium for it.

Pros
  • Up to 10,000MB/s read and write speeds
  • Stylish heatsink design
Cons
  • Faster Pro version is coming later this year
  • Expensive
  • No version without heatsink
$270 at Amazon $270 at Newegg

The MSI Spatium M570 uses the slightly slower 176-layer Micron NAND, which is rated at 1,600GB/s. That means it has a theoretical maximum of 10,000MB/s, and MSI has managed to squeeze every MB/s of performance out of the Phison E26 controller that every other drive on this list also uses. MSI is selling this SSD in 1TB, 2TB, and 4TB configurations, and a Pro version is coming later this year as well. Our testing showed max speeds of 10,084/10,201 MB/s read/writes, in line with the manufacturer's claims.

The Spatium M570 comes with an attached heatsink with stacked fins in an attractive bronze coloring. This is easily removable by unscrewing some bolts along the sides, so it is a good option whether your motherboard already has heatsinks built in. You'll want to keep that heatsink on or make sure the drive is cooled adequately, as our testing showed peaks of 81 Celsius, even with the heatsink on. The drives have up to 1,400TBW of endurance, with the 1TB model only having 700TBW, in line with other manufacturers’ endurance ratings. MSI is also giving these SSDs a five-year warranty.

It’s worth mentioning the upcoming M570 Pro model again, which will have sequential read speeds of up to 12,000MB/s and write speeds of up to 10,000/MB/s. That’s because it’s using the newer 232-layer Micron NAND, which has been in low supply until recently. If you really need the speed and like the look of the MSI, it's perhaps better to wait.

Source: Gigabyte
Gigabyte Aorus Gen5 10000
Best value

Fast and cheap

The Gigabyte Aorus Gen5 10000 was one of the first Gen5 SSDs to hit the market, and while it doesn't quite reach the top end it's a solid performer at 10,000MB/s speeds for reads and 9,500MB/s for writes. It also comes with an oversized heatsink comprised of heat pipes and fins, like a passive CPU cooler.

Pros
  • 10,000MB/s speeds are among the best
  • Black PCB
  • Optional heatsink
Cons
  • Heatsink is HUGE
  • No 4TB capacity option
$290 at Newegg

The Gigabyte Aorus Gen5 10000 is aptly named, given that it has sequential read and write speeds up to 10,000MB/s. It uses Phison’s E26 controller and the latest 232-layer 3D TLC NAND from Micron. This is the flash that has 2,400GB/s maximum speeds, although the company hasn’t gotten the full theoretical speeds on this drive. That will come when the Aorus Gen5 12000 is available later this year.

Gigabyte is currently selling 1TB and 2TB capacities of this drive, which come with a five-year warranty and 700TBW of endurance per 1TB of capacity. That’s on par with the competition. Gigabyte also supplies a mammoth heatsink with this drive, which has two heat pipes going into a large fin stack like on CPU air coolers. This is probably overkill, as most manufacturers are offering lower-profile aluminum heatsinks. Still, it’s nice to know that Gigabyte was thinking about heat, and lower heat overall will likely give your SSD a longer life span.

Source: Inland
Inland TD510 PCIe 5.0 NVMe
Best active cooling

Has a tiny fan

The Inland TD510 PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD has a 20mm thick aluminum heatsink with a small active fan for optimal cooling of the speedy PCIe 5.0 storage drive. It also has a 6-year limited warranty, one of the longest available.

Pros
  • Large heatsink with active fan cooling
  • DRAM cache
  • 6-year limited warranty
Cons
  • Speeds not near PCIe 5.0 maximums
  • Fan will likely be noisy
$154 at Amazon

Inland might not be a name you recognize, but you probably know of its parent company, MicroCenter. The in-house brand for SSDs is getting into the PCIe 5.0 market early with the Inland TD510. This model has sequential reads up to 10,000MB/s and writes up to 9,500MB/s. That’s not the fastest, but it’s also not the slowest, and this drive has other things going for it.

It was the first PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD on the market, beating the competition by a slim margin. The drive comes in an M.2 2280 single-sided form factor and comes in capacities of 1TB and 2TB. The chunky heatsink keeps things cool under sustained transfers and has a tiny fan that supposedly helps. It will be annoyingly noisy though, as tiny fans often are, so you might want to leave it unplugged. That heatsink can be removed if your motherboard already has integrated heatsinks.

This drive has a six-year warranty, which outpaces the average of five. It has an endurance rating of 700TBW per 1TB of capacity (meaning the 2TB model will have a 1400TBW endurance rating), which should mean this SSD will last you for a very long time before it needs replacing.

Source: Nextorage Japan
Nextorage Japan NVMe M.2 PCIe Gen5
Best heatsink

Designed to stay cool under pressure

The Nextorage Japan NVMe M.2 PCIe Gen5 has speeds of max read 9500MB/s and max write 8500MB/s, according to the company. It also has a five-year warranty and multiple on-device protections like bad block management and thermal throttling.

Pros
  • Huge, stylish heatsink
  • Relatively affordable
  • Five-year warranty
Cons
  • Heatsink might give installation issues on some builds
  • Speeds are on the slow side for PCIe 5.0
$170 at Newegg

The Nextorage Gen5 SSD NE5N Series is another PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD with a beefy heatsink. This drive has sequential read speeds of up to 10,000MB/s, and write speeds of up to 10,000MB/s, making it among the fastest on the market today. It also uses the M.2 2280 form factor, double-sided this time, with two flash packages, a DRAM cache, and a controller on the PCB.

What it does differently is with the heatsink. The dual-heatsink is designed to cool the NAND flash and the controller separately, allowing the two components to stay at a temperature they are comfortable at. The NAND is slightly more efficient while warm, so the larger heatsink is connected to the controller chip, where it will be more useful.

This drive has 700TBW per 1TB of capacity and is sold in 1TB and 2TB storage configurations. The 1TB drive is slightly slower, with sequential maximums of 9,500MB/s for reads and 8,500MB/s for writes. The DRAM cache also differs, as it uses 2GB of DDR4 per 1TB capacity, so the smaller drive capacity has 2GB, and the larger drive has 4GB. You also get a five-year warranty and a copy of Acronis True Image OEM for drive cloning from your existing device.

Source: Seagate
Seagate FireCuda 540 PCIe Gen5
Best for longevity

Dependable quality

$180 $190 Save $10

The Seagate FireCuda 540 PCIe Gen5 SSD is another one of the faster drives in this roundup, with up to 10,000MB/s for both read and write speeds. It's also rated for up to 2000TB total bytes written, which is a lifespan of 1TB of data rewritten daily for over five years (based on the 2TB capacity).

Pros
  • Up to 1.5 million IOPS 4KB Random Write
  • 10,000MB/s read and write speeds
  • Five-year warranty with 2000TB TBW
Cons
  • No heatsink option
  • Expensive
$181 at Newegg $180 at B&H

Seagate is one of the biggest names in storage, so it makes sense that it would be one of the early options in the PCIe 5.0 SSD market. The FireCuda 540 SSD is an M.2 2280 form-factor drive, only available without a heatsink option. It comes in 1TB and 2TB capacities, with the higher capacity reaching up to 10,000MB/s for sequential reads and writes. The smaller capacity caps out at 9,500MB/s for reads and 8,500MB/s for writes. Our testing showed max speeds of 10,065/10,199, which is in line with Seagate's claims.

This drive has a five-year warranty, but you might get to the end of that before you have any issues with your SSD. That’s because it has one of the highest endurance ratings of all the PCIe 5.0 SSDs, with 1000TBW per 1TB of capacity. That gives you 2000TBW for the 2TB capacity drive, or enough to rewrite 1TB of data every single day for over five years. That’s an extreme data transfer number, and most users won’t get anywhere near this figure with their daily use.

The only bad point here is that the price is similar to drives that come with a heatsink while not coming with a heatsink. Some users might appreciate not having to unbolt or unpeel a heatsink to fit their drive onto their motherboard, and I’m one of them. The ease of use of a bare NVMe SSD is more useful to me than having to figure out which M.2 slot I can use. You will want to cool the drive properly under sustained loads, as our testing showed temperatures of up to 86 C.

Source: Corsair
Corsair MP700 PCIe Gen5
Best customer support

From a trusted name

$150 $160 Save $10

The Corsair MP700 PCIe Gen5 SSD is rated for up to 9,500MB/s read speeds and 8,500MB/s write speeds, with up to 1.6 million 4KB random write IOPS. That's pretty speedy, with a five-year warranty to back it up.

Pros
  • Up to 10,000MB/s read / 9,500MB/s write speeds
  • 24/7 customer support and five-year warranty
  • More affordable than other options
Cons
  • Doesn't come with a heatsink
$150 at Newegg $150 at Corsair

The Corsair MP700 is the first PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD from the company. It is a barebones M.2 drive with the 2280 form factor, which means it doesn’t come with a heatsink. Maybe that’s okay because it’s the cheapest drive on this list while still keeping pace with the pack. Corsair also sells an NVMe waterblock if you are worried about temperatures or don’t have a motherboard with integrated heatsinks.

This drive comes in two capacities, 1TB and 2TB, both with sequential read speeds of up to 10,000MB/s. The 2TB matches that for write speeds, while the smaller capacity is slightly slower at 9,500MB/s. They also have 700TBW endurance per TB of capacity, so you don't lose out on longevity even with the lower price.

Corsair gives you a five-year warranty for this drive, matching the rest of the manufacturers. What gives this PCIe 5.0 extra value is the 24-hour, 7-days-a-week customer service that Corsair is known for, meaning if you have a problem, you won’t have to wait long to get it dealt with.

Source: fanxiang
Fanxiang S900 PCIe Gen5
Best no-frills

Fast performance and an included optional heatsink

The Fanxiang S900 PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD is a new name to the market, but it boasts the same specifications as the big-name drives. That's up to 10,000MB/s for reads and writes, 1.5 million IOPS, and a 2GB DRAM cache for sustained transfer speeds.

Pros
  • Comes with a heatsink
  • 10,000MB/s speeds
  • Five-year warranty
Cons
  • Relatively unknown brand
$190 at Amazon

The Fanxiang S900 PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD rounds up this list, being the only other PCIe 5.0 SSD that’s available to buy right now. The name isn’t one I was familiar with until now, but it comes with every specification of the better-known brands, so it deserves a place here.

The M.2 2280 form-factor drive has sequential read and write speeds of up to 10,000MB/s. It also has up to 1,500K IOPS for random read and write speeds. That’s on par with any drive here except for our top choice, the Crucial T700. It uses the latest 232-layer NAND flash and has 700TBW per 1TB of capacity.

You also get a separate heatsink, which you can apply if your motherboard doesn’t already have metal heatsinks. Fanxiang says this drive comes with a five-year warranty and has its own factory and service team in case something goes wrong.

What you need to know about the best PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSDs in 2023

The Crucial T700 Gen5 NVMe SSD is my recommendation for anyone buying a PCIe 5.0 SSD today. It has the fastest speeds available at 11,700MB/s sequential read, which is unsurprising since every other PCIe Gen5 manufacturer is using Micron’s 232-layer TLC NAND to make its SSDs. Keeping things in-house means Crucial could get more performance from Phison’s E26 controller. This is the drive to beat and only costs a few dollars more than the cheapest options.

That said, it costs more to get Crucial’s T700 with a heatsink, which is why value-orientated shoppers will want to look at other options, like Gigabyte’s Aorus Gen5 10000. This drive has slightly slower speeds, but you probably won’t notice that in everyday use, and the heatsink it comes with looks to dissipate the 11W power usage of the SSD easily. For a more stylish heatsink option, the MSI Spatium M570 is worth a look, especially if your PC is already full of other MSI hardware, as the designs will mesh together nicely.

Source: Crucial
Crucial T700 NVMe SSD
Editor's choice

Fastest drive on the market right now

$160 $180 Save $20

The Crucial T700 PCIe Gen5 NVMe SSD is one of the fastest solid-state drives on the planet, with up to 11,700MB/s reads and 9,500MB/s writes on the 1TB capacity, increasing to 12,400MB/s reads and 11,800MB/s writes on the larger capacities.

$160 at Amazon $160 at Best Buy $160 at Newegg

This is just the first round of releases for PCIe 5.0 SSDs, with over a dozen new models in the works from the usual suspects. While we’re only starting to test units, we have no doubt that at least some will end up as our favorite SSDs, purely from overall performance alone.
item.description:
The newest, fastest standard for M.2 SSDs, PCIe 5.0, has been around for a couple of years, but only this year brought a consumer release for the fastest M.2 SSDs. The first crop of M.2 NVMe SSDs using this standard promise speeds of 10,000MB/s or higher. When the second wave comes, expect speeds of 16,000MB/s, close to the theoretical limits of the PCIe 5.0 specification.

The only PCIe 5.0 SSDs available right now are from the first wave, and we’ve collected them all in one place to help you make your choice. Just keep in mind that you need a motherboard that supports PCIe 5.0, which is available on AMD’s AM5 platform, or some Intel motherboards that support 12-gen Intel CPUs and beyond.

  • Source: Crucial
    Crucial T700 NVMe SSD
    Editor's choice
    $160 at Amazon
  • Source: MSI
    MSI Spatium M570 PCIe 5.0 NVMe
    Premium pick
    $270 at Amazon
  • Source: Gigabyte
    Gigabyte Aorus Gen5 10000
    Best value
    $290 at Newegg
  • Source: Inland
    Inland TD510 PCIe 5.0 NVMe
    Best active cooling
    $154 at Amazon
  • Source: Nextorage Japan
    Nextorage Japan NVMe M.2 PCIe Gen5
    Best heatsink
    $170 at Newegg
  • Source: Seagate
    Seagate FireCuda 540 PCIe Gen5
    Best for longevity
    $180 at B&H
  • Source: Corsair
    Corsair MP700 PCIe Gen5
    Best customer support
    $150 at Newegg
  • Source: fanxiang
    Fanxiang S900 PCIe Gen5
    Best no-frills
    $190 at Amazon

These are the best PCIe 5.0 SSDs to buy in 2023

Source: Crucial
Crucial T700 NVMe SSD
Editor's choice

The fastest consumer SSD (for now)

$160 $180 Save $20

The Crucial T700 PCIe Gen5 NVMe SSD is one of the fastest solid-state drives on the planet, with up to 11,700MB/s reads and 9,500MB/s writes on the 1TB capacity, increasing to 12,400MB/s reads and 11,800MB/s writes on the larger capacities.

Pros
  • Blisteringly fast transfer speeds
  • Supports Microsoft DirectStorage
  • Optional heatsink
Cons
  • Not many devices support PCIe Gen 5 yet
  • Expensive compared to PCIe Gen 4 drives
$160 at Amazon $160 at Best Buy $160 at Newegg

The Crucial T700 is the best PCIe 5.0 SSD you can buy right now. That’s partly because it’s the fastest drive on the market, with 11,700MB/s max read speeds and 9,500MB/s write speeds in sequential transfers. It’s also partly because it’s one of the cheaper drives on the market, only a few dollars more per drive than the most affordable PCIe NVMe SSDs. Our testing showed max speeds of 12,398 MB/s reads and 11,814 MB/s writes, higher than Crucial's own seemingly conservative marketing numbers.

The drive comes in an M.2 2280 single-side form factor with an NVMe controller, a DRAM chip, and two flash packages. Those flash packages use Micron’s 232-layer TLC NAND3, which has a maximum speed of 2.4GB/s. Crucial has the edge as Micron is its parent company and gets access to the newer technology first, so it can optimize its SSDs accordingly.

The T700 drive is available in capacities of 1TB, 2TB, and 4TB, with or without heatsinks. We should mention that PCIe 5.0 SSDs run hot, and you will want a heatsink for them to work properly. The options with already-attached heatsinks are for motherboards that don’t already have onboard metal heatsinks to keep things cool. We noticed in our testing that the drive starts to thermal throttle under peak loads, with a temperature of 81 degrees Celsius. You'll want to keep this spicy drive well-cooled.

The drive also supports Microsoft’s DirectStorage, which enables your graphics card and SSD to transfer data to each other without it first going through the CPU. This reduces loading times for textures and other assets while lowering CPU utilization. Only one current game supports this, Forsaken, but others are coming soon. It's backed by a five-year warranty, which seems to be the standard for this gen.

Source: MSI
MSI Spatium M570 PCIe 5.0 NVMe
Premium pick

Eye-catching design

$270 $350 Save $80

The MSI Spatium M570 PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD is a premium M.2 SSD with blisteringly fast 10,000MB/s read and write speeds using the PCIe 5.0 standard. It comes with a chunky aluminum heatsink to keep things cool, though you pay a premium for it.

Pros
  • Up to 10,000MB/s read and write speeds
  • Stylish heatsink design
Cons
  • Faster Pro version is coming later this year
  • Expensive
  • No version without heatsink
$270 at Amazon $270 at Newegg

The MSI Spatium M570 uses the slightly slower 176-layer Micron NAND, which is rated at 1,600GB/s. That means it has a theoretical maximum of 10,000MB/s, and MSI has managed to squeeze every MB/s of performance out of the Phison E26 controller that every other drive on this list also uses. MSI is selling this SSD in 1TB, 2TB, and 4TB configurations, and a Pro version is coming later this year as well. Our testing showed max speeds of 10,084/10,201 MB/s read/writes, in line with the manufacturer's claims.

The Spatium M570 comes with an attached heatsink with stacked fins in an attractive bronze coloring. This is easily removable by unscrewing some bolts along the sides, so it is a good option whether your motherboard already has heatsinks built in. You'll want to keep that heatsink on or make sure the drive is cooled adequately, as our testing showed peaks of 81 Celsius, even with the heatsink on. The drives have up to 1,400TBW of endurance, with the 1TB model only having 700TBW, in line with other manufacturers’ endurance ratings. MSI is also giving these SSDs a five-year warranty.

It’s worth mentioning the upcoming M570 Pro model again, which will have sequential read speeds of up to 12,000MB/s and write speeds of up to 10,000/MB/s. That’s because it’s using the newer 232-layer Micron NAND, which has been in low supply until recently. If you really need the speed and like the look of the MSI, it's perhaps better to wait.

Source: Gigabyte
Gigabyte Aorus Gen5 10000
Best value

Fast and cheap

The Gigabyte Aorus Gen5 10000 was one of the first Gen5 SSDs to hit the market, and while it doesn't quite reach the top end it's a solid performer at 10,000MB/s speeds for reads and 9,500MB/s for writes. It also comes with an oversized heatsink comprised of heat pipes and fins, like a passive CPU cooler.

Pros
  • 10,000MB/s speeds are among the best
  • Black PCB
  • Optional heatsink
Cons
  • Heatsink is HUGE
  • No 4TB capacity option
$290 at Newegg

The Gigabyte Aorus Gen5 10000 is aptly named, given that it has sequential read and write speeds up to 10,000MB/s. It uses Phison’s E26 controller and the latest 232-layer 3D TLC NAND from Micron. This is the flash that has 2,400GB/s maximum speeds, although the company hasn’t gotten the full theoretical speeds on this drive. That will come when the Aorus Gen5 12000 is available later this year.

Gigabyte is currently selling 1TB and 2TB capacities of this drive, which come with a five-year warranty and 700TBW of endurance per 1TB of capacity. That’s on par with the competition. Gigabyte also supplies a mammoth heatsink with this drive, which has two heat pipes going into a large fin stack like on CPU air coolers. This is probably overkill, as most manufacturers are offering lower-profile aluminum heatsinks. Still, it’s nice to know that Gigabyte was thinking about heat, and lower heat overall will likely give your SSD a longer life span.

Source: Inland
Inland TD510 PCIe 5.0 NVMe
Best active cooling

Has a tiny fan

The Inland TD510 PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD has a 20mm thick aluminum heatsink with a small active fan for optimal cooling of the speedy PCIe 5.0 storage drive. It also has a 6-year limited warranty, one of the longest available.

Pros
  • Large heatsink with active fan cooling
  • DRAM cache
  • 6-year limited warranty
Cons
  • Speeds not near PCIe 5.0 maximums
  • Fan will likely be noisy
$154 at Amazon

Inland might not be a name you recognize, but you probably know of its parent company, MicroCenter. The in-house brand for SSDs is getting into the PCIe 5.0 market early with the Inland TD510. This model has sequential reads up to 10,000MB/s and writes up to 9,500MB/s. That’s not the fastest, but it’s also not the slowest, and this drive has other things going for it.

It was the first PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD on the market, beating the competition by a slim margin. The drive comes in an M.2 2280 single-sided form factor and comes in capacities of 1TB and 2TB. The chunky heatsink keeps things cool under sustained transfers and has a tiny fan that supposedly helps. It will be annoyingly noisy though, as tiny fans often are, so you might want to leave it unplugged. That heatsink can be removed if your motherboard already has integrated heatsinks.

This drive has a six-year warranty, which outpaces the average of five. It has an endurance rating of 700TBW per 1TB of capacity (meaning the 2TB model will have a 1400TBW endurance rating), which should mean this SSD will last you for a very long time before it needs replacing.

Source: Nextorage Japan
Nextorage Japan NVMe M.2 PCIe Gen5
Best heatsink

Designed to stay cool under pressure

The Nextorage Japan NVMe M.2 PCIe Gen5 has speeds of max read 9500MB/s and max write 8500MB/s, according to the company. It also has a five-year warranty and multiple on-device protections like bad block management and thermal throttling.

Pros
  • Huge, stylish heatsink
  • Relatively affordable
  • Five-year warranty
Cons
  • Heatsink might give installation issues on some builds
  • Speeds are on the slow side for PCIe 5.0
$170 at Newegg

The Nextorage Gen5 SSD NE5N Series is another PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD with a beefy heatsink. This drive has sequential read speeds of up to 10,000MB/s, and write speeds of up to 10,000MB/s, making it among the fastest on the market today. It also uses the M.2 2280 form factor, double-sided this time, with two flash packages, a DRAM cache, and a controller on the PCB.

What it does differently is with the heatsink. The dual-heatsink is designed to cool the NAND flash and the controller separately, allowing the two components to stay at a temperature they are comfortable at. The NAND is slightly more efficient while warm, so the larger heatsink is connected to the controller chip, where it will be more useful.

This drive has 700TBW per 1TB of capacity and is sold in 1TB and 2TB storage configurations. The 1TB drive is slightly slower, with sequential maximums of 9,500MB/s for reads and 8,500MB/s for writes. The DRAM cache also differs, as it uses 2GB of DDR4 per 1TB capacity, so the smaller drive capacity has 2GB, and the larger drive has 4GB. You also get a five-year warranty and a copy of Acronis True Image OEM for drive cloning from your existing device.

Source: Seagate
Seagate FireCuda 540 PCIe Gen5
Best for longevity

Dependable quality

$180 $190 Save $10

The Seagate FireCuda 540 PCIe Gen5 SSD is another one of the faster drives in this roundup, with up to 10,000MB/s for both read and write speeds. It's also rated for up to 2000TB total bytes written, which is a lifespan of 1TB of data rewritten daily for over five years (based on the 2TB capacity).

Pros
  • Up to 1.5 million IOPS 4KB Random Write
  • 10,000MB/s read and write speeds
  • Five-year warranty with 2000TB TBW
Cons
  • No heatsink option
  • Expensive
$181 at Newegg $180 at B&H

Seagate is one of the biggest names in storage, so it makes sense that it would be one of the early options in the PCIe 5.0 SSD market. The FireCuda 540 SSD is an M.2 2280 form-factor drive, only available without a heatsink option. It comes in 1TB and 2TB capacities, with the higher capacity reaching up to 10,000MB/s for sequential reads and writes. The smaller capacity caps out at 9,500MB/s for reads and 8,500MB/s for writes. Our testing showed max speeds of 10,065/10,199, which is in line with Seagate's claims.

This drive has a five-year warranty, but you might get to the end of that before you have any issues with your SSD. That’s because it has one of the highest endurance ratings of all the PCIe 5.0 SSDs, with 1000TBW per 1TB of capacity. That gives you 2000TBW for the 2TB capacity drive, or enough to rewrite 1TB of data every single day for over five years. That’s an extreme data transfer number, and most users won’t get anywhere near this figure with their daily use.

The only bad point here is that the price is similar to drives that come with a heatsink while not coming with a heatsink. Some users might appreciate not having to unbolt or unpeel a heatsink to fit their drive onto their motherboard, and I’m one of them. The ease of use of a bare NVMe SSD is more useful to me than having to figure out which M.2 slot I can use. You will want to cool the drive properly under sustained loads, as our testing showed temperatures of up to 86 C.

Source: Corsair
Corsair MP700 PCIe Gen5
Best customer support

From a trusted name

$150 $160 Save $10

The Corsair MP700 PCIe Gen5 SSD is rated for up to 9,500MB/s read speeds and 8,500MB/s write speeds, with up to 1.6 million 4KB random write IOPS. That's pretty speedy, with a five-year warranty to back it up.

Pros
  • Up to 10,000MB/s read / 9,500MB/s write speeds
  • 24/7 customer support and five-year warranty
  • More affordable than other options
Cons
  • Doesn't come with a heatsink
$150 at Newegg $150 at Corsair

The Corsair MP700 is the first PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD from the company. It is a barebones M.2 drive with the 2280 form factor, which means it doesn’t come with a heatsink. Maybe that’s okay because it’s the cheapest drive on this list while still keeping pace with the pack. Corsair also sells an NVMe waterblock if you are worried about temperatures or don’t have a motherboard with integrated heatsinks.

This drive comes in two capacities, 1TB and 2TB, both with sequential read speeds of up to 10,000MB/s. The 2TB matches that for write speeds, while the smaller capacity is slightly slower at 9,500MB/s. They also have 700TBW endurance per TB of capacity, so you don't lose out on longevity even with the lower price.

Corsair gives you a five-year warranty for this drive, matching the rest of the manufacturers. What gives this PCIe 5.0 extra value is the 24-hour, 7-days-a-week customer service that Corsair is known for, meaning if you have a problem, you won’t have to wait long to get it dealt with.

Source: fanxiang
Fanxiang S900 PCIe Gen5
Best no-frills

Fast performance and an included optional heatsink

The Fanxiang S900 PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD is a new name to the market, but it boasts the same specifications as the big-name drives. That's up to 10,000MB/s for reads and writes, 1.5 million IOPS, and a 2GB DRAM cache for sustained transfer speeds.

Pros
  • Comes with a heatsink
  • 10,000MB/s speeds
  • Five-year warranty
Cons
  • Relatively unknown brand
$190 at Amazon

The Fanxiang S900 PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD rounds up this list, being the only other PCIe 5.0 SSD that’s available to buy right now. The name isn’t one I was familiar with until now, but it comes with every specification of the better-known brands, so it deserves a place here.

The M.2 2280 form-factor drive has sequential read and write speeds of up to 10,000MB/s. It also has up to 1,500K IOPS for random read and write speeds. That’s on par with any drive here except for our top choice, the Crucial T700. It uses the latest 232-layer NAND flash and has 700TBW per 1TB of capacity.

You also get a separate heatsink, which you can apply if your motherboard doesn’t already have metal heatsinks. Fanxiang says this drive comes with a five-year warranty and has its own factory and service team in case something goes wrong.

What you need to know about the best PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSDs in 2023

The Crucial T700 Gen5 NVMe SSD is my recommendation for anyone buying a PCIe 5.0 SSD today. It has the fastest speeds available at 11,700MB/s sequential read, which is unsurprising since every other PCIe Gen5 manufacturer is using Micron’s 232-layer TLC NAND to make its SSDs. Keeping things in-house means Crucial could get more performance from Phison’s E26 controller. This is the drive to beat and only costs a few dollars more than the cheapest options.

That said, it costs more to get Crucial’s T700 with a heatsink, which is why value-orientated shoppers will want to look at other options, like Gigabyte’s Aorus Gen5 10000. This drive has slightly slower speeds, but you probably won’t notice that in everyday use, and the heatsink it comes with looks to dissipate the 11W power usage of the SSD easily. For a more stylish heatsink option, the MSI Spatium M570 is worth a look, especially if your PC is already full of other MSI hardware, as the designs will mesh together nicely.

Source: Crucial
Crucial T700 NVMe SSD
Editor's choice

Fastest drive on the market right now

$160 $180 Save $20

The Crucial T700 PCIe Gen5 NVMe SSD is one of the fastest solid-state drives on the planet, with up to 11,700MB/s reads and 9,500MB/s writes on the 1TB capacity, increasing to 12,400MB/s reads and 11,800MB/s writes on the larger capacities.

$160 at Amazon $160 at Best Buy $160 at Newegg

This is just the first round of releases for PCIe 5.0 SSDs, with over a dozen new models in the works from the usual suspects. While we’re only starting to test units, we have no doubt that at least some will end up as our favorite SSDs, purely from overall performance alone.
